title: Single Spatio-Temporal Mode Bright Twin-Beam Source Across the Near- and Mid-Infrared
authors: Gabriel Demontigny, Patrick Cusson, Amauri Perraton Elorza, Esteban Murillo Zapata, Eli Martel, Andrei Rasputnyi, Maria Chekhova, Stéphane Virally, Denis Seletskiy
abstract: We introduce an ultrafast, bright, entangled twin-beam source generated by type-0 parametric down-conversion in periodically-poled lithium niobate at MHz repetition rate, with continuously tunable Schmidt number $K$ set by the pump pulse duration. Photon-number statistics characterization via $g^{(2)}(0)$ and singular-value decomposition of the signal spectral density matrix yield $K\simeq1.05$ and $K\simeq1.03$, respectively, maintained over multiple orders of magnitude in brightness. Group-delay dispersion of the pump drives a continuous transition from single-mode operation to a controlled multimode regime, consistent with the temporal gain window departing from the inverse phase-matching bandwidth. Strong non-degeneracy of the source (signal at 1.37 um, idler at 4.0 um, $\sim 100$ fs duration) decouples a mid-infrared interaction wavelength, which overlaps with molecular vibrational resonances, from a near-infrared detection band, establishing a practical platform for quantum-enhanced metrology, nonlinear interferometry, and mid-infrared spectroscopic sensing. We show that in the bright few-mode limit, the total entanglement resource is clearly separated between modal and occupational degrees of freedom, and that our source allocates up to 95-97% of that resource to the occupational sector.
summary: The authors report on the creation of a bright, entangled twin-beam source using PDC in PPLN, demonstrating precise control over its spatio-temporal mode structure. By tuning the pump pulse duration via dispersion, they can transition between single-mode and controlled multimode operation. This platform is highly valuable for quantum-enhanced metrology due to its strong non-degeneracy and quantifiable entanglement resource.
main_problem: The goal is to develop and characterize an ultrafast, bright, entangled twin-beam source that maintains spatio-temporal mode control across varying brightness levels.
main_result: The source successfully operates in a near-single spatio-temporal mode regime ($K \approx 1$) and establishes a practical platform for quantum-enhanced metrology due to strong non-degeneracy.
method: Characterization involves measuring photon-number statistics via $g^{(2)}(0)$ and performing singular-value decomposition (Schmidt decomposition) on the spectral density matrix.
model_or_system: The system uses type-0 parametric down-conversion (PDC) in a periodically-poled lithium niobate (PPLN) crystal to generate entangled signal (near-IR) and idler (mid-IR) beams.
key_observables: Second-order correlation function $g^{(2)}(0)$, Schmidt numbers ($K$), and the separation of entanglement resource into modal and occupational degrees of freedom.
important_parameters: Pump pulse duration ($	au_p$), Group-Delay Dispersion (GDD), and the resulting effective mode number $K$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the Schmidt decomposition of the Joint Spectral Amplitude (JSA) and assumes the validity of the Bloch-Messiah decomposition in the bright limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup using PPLN, show the dependence of signal photons on pump photons, and plot photon number histograms and $g^{(2)}(0)$ vs. mode entropy.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from introducing the physical platform (PDC in PPLN) to detailing the diagnostics ($g^{(2)}(0)$, SVD), establishing the control mechanism (GDD tuning), and finally quantifying the entanglement resource distribution.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly controllable, broadband source of entangled photons, which is crucial for advancing quantum sensing and nonlinear quantum optics experiments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. Generation of ultrafast bright entangled twin-beams. a) Experimental setup. A pump beam (1026 nm, 260 fs, 5 µJ) is attenuated with a neutral density filter (ND1) and focused into a periodically-poled Lithium Niobate (PPLN) crystal of 2-mm length with a 27.91 µm poling period, using a lens (L1) with 250 mm focal length. A mirror (M1) reflects the beam back into the crystal for additional nonlinear interaction. The PPLN is positioned so that the pump’s focal plane is at the second pass. The beam is collimated by a concave mirror (CM1) with a focal length of 150 mm. The signal twin (1.37 µm, 100 fs), idler twin (4.0 µm, 100 fs) and pump are separated using a pair of dichroic mirrors...

Figure 1a shows the experimental setup used to generate ultra- fast bright entangled twin beams via type-0 SPDC in a PPLN crystal. The pump is provided by a Yb:KGW (Light Conversion Pharos) laser (1026 nm central wavelength, 260 fs pulse duration, 5 µJ pulse energy, up to 1 MHz repetition rate) and is attenuated using a reflective neutral density filter (ND1) before being fo- cused into the PPLN crystal using a 250 mm focal-length lens (L1). The PPLN poling period is 27.91 µm and is chosen to produce strongly non-degenerate output with the signal beam centered at 1.37 µm and the idler beam at 4.0 µm (both ∼100 fs duration).
